summary: Atwood argues that developers should prioritize books focused on ideas and theory over thick technical manuals, as conceptual knowledge has a much longer shelf life than tool-specific documentation. He points to The Mythical Man Month as an example of a 20-year-old book that remains more valuable than current technical tomes. The post also explores the meta-skill of learning to learn, emphasizing the importance of asking 'why' repeatedly until reaching foundational knowledge. Atwood concludes with a critique of overly complex systems like COM, suggesting that truly well-designed systems should be easy to understand without extensive training.
key_points: Books heavy on ideas and light on code have longer shelf lives and greater impact than tool-specific manuals
Theory and knowledge change slowly and incrementally, making them timeless investments
Effective learning requires connecting new information to existing knowledge through persistent questioning
Well-designed systems should be easy to understand - complexity requiring week-long training courses indicates poor design
The ability to learn how to learn may be more valuable than any specific technical knowledge
money_quotes: Books that are heavy on ideas and light on code end up having a longer shelf-life and make more of an impact.
Knowledge and theory never goes out of style, unless you stop improving on it.
The most important thing that I've ever learned is how to learn.
All learning is only as effective as well as it can be tied to what you already know.
The more you know, the easier it is to learn more.
Well designed systems are easy to understand.
Anyone can develop a complex system that takes a week long training course to comprehend. Developing a complex system that a developer can master in a day, well, that's a lot more impressive.
key_insight: Investing in timeless conceptual knowledge and the meta-skill of learning yields far greater returns than chasing the latest technical documentation.
